text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Hospitalization Visit — Immune-Mediated Colitis Recovery / Immunotherapy Resumption Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old female with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma who presents for oncology follow-up approximately 2 weeks following hospital discharge after a grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring IV methylprednisolone and infliximab. She is here to assess symptom resolution, complete steroid taper, and discuss the plan for resuming immunotherapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu's oncologic history is well known to this service. She was diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma approximately 14 months ago with bulky left axillary adenopathy (4.8 cm), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm), and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the left flank. LDH was markedly elevated at 2.4x ULN at diagnosis. She completed comprehensive molecular profiling which confirmed BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%), co-occurring NF1 p.Q1300* loss-of-function mutation, high TMB (42 mutations/Mb), and PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative.

She was initiated on dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg once daily as first-line therapy and had an excellent initial response — LDH normalized and imaging at 8 weeks showed approximately 60% reduction in tumor burden. She experienced a brief febrile episode at approximately 3 weeks (grade 1 pyrexia requiring 3-day dabrafenib hold) and later developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia at approximately 5 months (trametinib dose reduced to 1.5 mg, symptoms resolved to grade 1, trametinib re-escalated). Disease remained well-controlled through the 6-month scan.

Unfortunately, at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib, restaging CT demonstrated clear progression — re-enlargement of the left axillary mass to 3.4 cm with necrosis, growth of hepatic lesions with two new hepatic lesions, re-enlargement of the left flank subcutaneous nodule, and a new right inguinal lymph node. LDH rose to 285 U/L. Brain MRI at the time of progression was again negative. In light of the progression on BRAF/MEK inhibition, the patient's high TMB, and meaningful PD-L1 expression, she was transitioned to second-line combination checkpoint immunotherapy: ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks, planned for 4 cycles of induction followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks as maintenance.

She received Cycle 1 of ipi/nivo without acute infusion reactions and was given Cycle 2 on schedule. Approximately 5 weeks after starting (roughly 2 weeks after Cycle 2), she developed 10-12 watery/loose bowel movements per day with crampy lower abdominal pain, hematochezia, and low-grade fever. Stool cultures and C. diff were negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrated diffuse mucosal erythema, edema, and focal ulcerations from the rectum to the descending colon; biopsies confirmed active colitis with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. She was admitted and started on methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day IV. After 3 days without adequate improvement, infliximab 5 mg/kg IV was administered as a single dose. Bowel symptoms responded dramatically within 48 hours of infliximab. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held. She was transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and discharged in stable condition. GI was involved throughout her hospitalization.

Today she returns for post-discharge follow-up. She reports that her bowel function has fully returned to baseline — she is having 1 to 2 well-formed stools per day with no blood, no urgency, and no cramping. She has completed approximately 8 weeks of steroid taper per schedule, having tapered from prednisone 1 mg/kg down to 10 mg daily over the preceding 4 weeks, with a plan to discontinue steroids completely over the next 1-2 weeks as tolerated. She has been off steroids for approximately 10 days at this visit. She notes mild fatigue that has been slowly improving, and she endorses mild cold intolerance over the past few weeks — she has been wearing an extra layer at home. No new rashes. No visual changes. No arthralgias or myalgias. No headache or new neurological symptoms. She lost approximately 4 lbs during the hospitalization but reports she has been eating normally since discharge. Appetite is back. No nausea, vomiting, or dysphagia.

She has a scheduled return to work (high school science teacher) planned for next month. She reports her mood is "cautiously optimistic" and is asking when she can restart her immunotherapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue improving. No fevers, chills, or sweats. Weight loss of approximately 4 lbs during hospitalization; recovering appetite since discharge.
Skin: No new rashes. No palmar-plantar symptoms at this visit. No hyperpigmentation or vitiligo (to patient's knowledge).
Eyes: No visual changes, no diplopia, no eye pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function returned to baseline as noted. No abdominal pain. No hematochezia. No rectal urgency.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias, no myalgias, no joint swelling.
Neurological: No headache, no focal weakness, no sensory changes, no cognitive changes.
Endocrine: Mild cold intolerance noted (new symptom, see below). No polyuria or polydipsia. No heat intolerance.
Psychiatric: Mild situational anxiety about cancer status and immunotherapy resumption; otherwise normal.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Somewhat pale in appearance compared to prior visits. Dressed warmly for the ambient clinic temperature.
Vital signs: Temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 68 bpm, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 62.3 kg (baseline approximately 66 kg; down approximately 4 lbs since last pre-admission visit).
Skin: No jaundice, no icterus. No new cutaneous melanocytic lesions suspicious for recurrence on gross inspection. No palmar-palmar erythema. No vitiligo or depigmented patches noted on visible skin. No new subcutaneous nodules palpable.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Oropharynx clear, no mucosal lesions. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No thyroid enlargement on palpation; thyroid is smooth, non-tender, non-nodular.
Chest/Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No guarding or rebound. No hepatomegaly on palpation. Normal bowel sounds. No right upper quadrant tenderness to deep palpation.
Lymph nodes: Left axilla — a firm but somewhat smaller lymph node mass is palpable, estimated approximately 2.5 to 3 cm; difficult to assess definitively on exam given no recent restaging imaging. Right axilla — no lymphadenopathy. Right inguinal — trace fullness palpable, uncertain clinical significance, likely small node. Bilateral inguinal — no clearly enlarged nodes.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count: WBC 6.2 x10^9/L (differential: neutrophils 62%, lymphocytes 28%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 0%); hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild, likely related to nutritional recovery and recent illness); platelets 234 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 92 mg/dL.
Liver function tests: Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, albumin 3.7 g/dL — all within normal limits, representing normalization from the mildly elevated transaminases noted at progression.
LDH: 178 U/L (just at upper limit of normal — slight elevation from 148 U/L at the prior check, though within acceptable range; will monitor).
C-reactive protein: 4.2 mg/L (normalized from markedly elevated values during hospitalization, near resolution of systemic inflammation).
Thyroid function tests: TSH 5.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated; normal range 0.4–4.5 mIU/L), free T4 0.92 ng/dL (low-normal). Note: TSH was not checked at the time of the colitis admission. This represents a new finding. Given the timing — approximately 8–10 weeks after starting ipi/nivo — immune-related thyroiditis with subclinical hypothyroidism must be considered and is high on the differential.
Corticotropin (ACTH) stimulation not performed at this visit; patient is currently completing final prednisone taper and adrenal function will be reassessed if symptoms develop after full steroid discontinuation.
Stool calprotectin: Pending at time of note.

---

IMAGING / OTHER DATA REVIEW
No restaging imaging has been performed since the hospitalization. The most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis was the progression scan at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib, showing left axillary node 3.4 cm, segment VI hepatic lesion 2.6 cm with new segment II (1.5 cm) and segment IVa (1.2 cm) lesions, subcutaneous nodule 1.4 cm, and right inguinal node 1.2 cm, with LDH 285 U/L. Given that the patient only completed 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab prior to the colitis-related hold, and has been on steroids for the past 8+ weeks, no meaningful assessment of immunotherapy response is yet possible by imaging. Restaging CT will be planned once the patient has been off steroids for at least 2–4 weeks and has had adequate time on nivolumab monotherapy.
Brain MRI at the time of progression was negative for intracranial metastases; no new neurological symptoms to prompt repeat at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma — status post progression on dabrafenib/trametinib, 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab completed (held for grade 3 immune colitis). Disease status by imaging currently uncertain given interval since last scan; clinical exam mildly reassuring (no dramatic new masses) but not definitive.

   - Given the severity of the grade 3 colitis, ipilimumab is permanently discontinued. The patient received only 2 of the planned 4 induction cycles; however, restarting ipilimumab in the setting of prior grade 3 immune colitis requiring infliximab is not appropriate per current guidelines and institutional practice.
   - Plan to resume nivolumab (Opdivo) monotherapy at 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance dosing) once the following criteria are met: (1) colitis fully resolved (bowel function at baseline — met today), (2) patient off systemic corticosteroids for at least 2 weeks (anticipated within the next 10–14 days with the final prednisone taper), and (3) no other active immune-related adverse events. Based on today's assessment, if the final prednisone taper proceeds as planned, the patient should be eligible to resume nivolumab in approximately 3 to 4 weeks.
   - Patient was counseled that single-agent nivolumab, while representing a reduction in the intensity of the checkpoint inhibitor combination, still has meaningful activity in BRAF-mutant melanoma, and given this patient's very high TMB (42 mut/Mb) and significant PD-L1 expression (CPS 55), there is a reasonable basis for expecting benefit from anti-PD-1 monotherapy even after the abbreviated ipi/nivo induction.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be scheduled approximately 8–12 weeks after resuming nivolumab. We will not scan at this time given the patient is completing a steroid course and clinically appears stable.
   - Patient was counseled to watch closely for any signs of colitis recurrence once nivolumab is restarted (increased stool frequency, blood in stool, cramping) and to contact the oncology nurse triage line immediately if these symptoms develop. GI is aware of the plan to rechallenge with single-agent anti-PD-1 and is available for consultation.

2. Grade 3 immune-mediated colitis — resolving. Bowel function at baseline today. Completing final prednisone taper (currently at 5 mg daily, plan to discontinue in 1 week). Patient instructed to take the taper as follows: 5 mg daily x7 days, then stop, with follow-up call in 1 week. CRP normalizing. Patient to avoid NSAIDs. Continue high-fiber, bowel-friendly diet. Gastroenterology follow-up appointment confirmed in 6 weeks. Will check stool calprotectin at next visit.

3. Thyroid function — subclinical hypothyroidism, likely immune-mediated (immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroiditis). TSH 5.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4, in the setting of mild cold intolerance and fatigue. This is consistent with evolving immunotherapy-associated thyroiditis, which is a well-recognized adverse effect of anti-PD-1 therapy. The patient was informed of this finding.
   - Will recheck TSH and free T4 in 4 weeks (approximately at the time of the next visit).
   - If TSH remains elevated or rises further, or if free T4 declines below the normal range, will initiate levothyroxine replacement. At this point, given that TSH is only mildly elevated (5.8) and free T4 remains within normal limits (though at the low-normal end), a watchful waiting approach is reasonable for the next 4 weeks. Patient was counseled to report worsening fatigue, significant cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, or any other hypothyroid symptoms.
   - Endocrinology referral is not required at this point; will manage thyroid issues in the oncology setting and consult endocrinology if the situation becomes more complex.

4. Mild anemia — hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, likely multifactorial (recent acute illness, nutritional depletion, possible mild anemia of chronic disease). Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered. Will recheck CBC at next visit. No symptoms of severe anemia; no transfusion indicated at this time. Encourage iron-rich diet and nutritional recovery.

5. Nutrition and weight — the patient lost approximately 4 lbs during the acute colitis episode. Appetite has returned to normal. Encouraged to continue working on nutritional recovery. Dietitian referral placed.

6. Dysplastic nevus syndrome — ongoing dermatology surveillance. Dermatology appointment confirmed; patient reminded to perform monthly self-skin exams and to report any changing lesions.

7. Psychosocial — patient reports mild anxiety related to uncertainty about disease status and immunotherapy resumption. She was provided with reassurance and thorough explanation of the next steps. Social work referral offered; patient declined at this time, stating her family support system is strong. She expressed eagerness to return to work and was encouraged.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks (or sooner if concerns arise), at which time we anticipate the patient will have completed steroid taper, will be eligible to resume nivolumab, and we will recheck thyroid function. Orders placed for TSH/free T4 in 4 weeks, CBC in 4 weeks, iron studies today, and restaging CT approximately 8 weeks after nivolumab resumption.

Time spent with patient: 45 minutes (>50% of this time involved discussion of immunotherapy resumption plan, immune-related adverse event management, and thyroid findings). Attestation and signature on file.

— Dictated and signed by attending oncologist
